name: Navajas — Circular to Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan
name_original: Navajas — Circular al Salto de la Novia y la Cascada del Tío Juan
region_intro: Hiking around Navajas offers diverse routes through a landscape characterized by significant water features, including waterfalls, springs, and river valleys. The region features the historic Ojos Negros Greenway, providing accessible paths along a former railway line. Terrain varies from gentle riverside walks to paths with moderate elevation changes, suitable for different hiking preferences. This area in Castellón, Spain, is known for its natural beauty and well-maintained trails. Best hiking trails around Navajas The most popular hiking route is Route of the Springs: Navajas to Segorbe, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 55 minutes to complete. This route follows a scenic path connecting two towns, highlighting the area's natural springs. Another top favourite among local hikers is Navajas — Circular to Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan, an easy 2.5 miles (4.0 km) path. This circular route explores two notable waterfalls, Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan, offering picturesque views. Local hikers also love the Ojos Negros Greenway – Fuente de los Baños loop from Navajas, a 5.8 miles (9.3 km) trail leading through a former railway line and past natural springs, often completed in about 2 hours 33 minutes. Hiking around Navajas is defined by its waterfalls, natural springs, and the Ojos Negros Greenway, offering a mix of flat paths and gentle ascents. The network offers options for easy family walks as well as more moderate routes. The routes in Navajas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 150 reviews. More than 600 hikers have used komoot to explore Navajas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Navajas?
A: Hiking around Navajas offers a varied landscape, characterized by significant water features like waterfalls, springs, and river valleys. You'll find paths ranging from gentle riverside walks to routes with moderate elevation changes. The historic Ojos Negros Greenway provides accessible, flatter paths along a former railway line, while other trails involve more natural, uneven ground.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls in Navajas?
A: Yes, Navajas is well-known for its waterfalls. You can explore trails that lead to prominent cascades such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1310997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bride's Leap Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2099448" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tío Juan Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e879013197/navajas-circular-to-salto-de-la-novia-and-cascada-del-tio-juan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navajas — Circular to Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan&lt;/a&gt;, which visits both.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Navajas?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e879013197/navajas-circular-to-salto-de-la-novia-and-cascada-del-tio-juan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navajas — Circular to Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4 km loop. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31475280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos Negros Greenway – Fuente de los Baños loop from Navajas&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 9.3 km trail.

Q: Are there easy or family-friendly hiking options in Navajas?
A: Absolutely. Navajas offers a good selection of easy and family-friendly routes. Out of 37 available tours, 14 are classified as easy. The trails along the Ojos Negros Greenway are generally flat and accessible, suitable for all ages. The 4 km &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e879013197/navajas-circular-to-salto-de-la-novia-and-cascada-del-tio-juan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navajas — Circular to Salto de la Novia and Cascada del Tío Juan&lt;/a&gt; is also an easy option.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks to see while hiking?
A: Beyond the waterfalls, Navajas is rich in natural features. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2698324" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fountain of the 50 Spouts&lt;/a&gt;, a unique natural monument. The Ojos Negros Greenway itself is a historic landmark, offering scenic views along its path. The region also features numerous natural springs and river landscapes.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Navajas?
A: The hiking trails in Navajas are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 150 reviews. Hikers often praise the well-maintained paths, the abundance of natural springs and waterfalls, and the diverse scenery that makes each route unique.

Q: Is there public transport available to reach the trailheads in Navajas?
A: Navajas is a small town, and while local buses might connect it to larger towns, direct public transport to specific trailheads outside the town center can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for connections to Navajas itself, and then plan to walk or use a short taxi ride to reach more remote starting points.

Q: Where can I park my car when hiking in Navajas?
A: Navajas typically has public parking areas within or on the outskirts of the town. For popular trailheads, especially those near attractions like Salto de la Novia, designated parking lots are usually available. It's recommended to arrive early during peak seasons to secure a spot.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Navajas?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on most hiking trails in Navajas, especially if kept on a leash. It's always a good idea to bring water for your dog, particularly during warmer months, and to be mindful of local regulations or signage that might indicate restrictions in certain protected areas or near specific attractions.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Navajas?
A: The best time to hike in Navajas is typically during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is mild and pleasant, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summers can be hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winters are generally mild but can be damp.

Q: Are there any long-distance hiking options or multi-day routes in Navajas?
A: While most routes directly from Navajas are day hikes, the region connects to longer networks. The Ojos Negros Greenway, for instance, is part of a much longer former railway line that stretches for many kilometers, offering potential for extended walks or cycling trips beyond the immediate Navajas area. Individual routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3962367" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bride's Leap Waterfall – View of Regajo Dam loop from Navajas&lt;/a&gt; can be up to 13.5 km.

Q: Where can I find places to eat or drink near the hiking trails?
A: Navajas town center offers various cafes, restaurants, and bars where you can refuel before or after your hike. For trails that pass through other villages or popular spots like the Salto de la Novia area, you might find small kiosks or establishments offering refreshments. It's always wise to carry snacks and water, especially on longer routes.
tips: Beautiful small waterfall, accessible via stairs.

This beautiful 30m waterfall owes its name to its similarity to a bride's veil, although history hides a tragic legend.
They say in the town that when a couple wanted to get married, they had to go to the area where the river narrowed and once there, the girl would jump from one bank to the other. Before the eyes of the public, if everything went well, it was believed that the marriage would be prosperous, otherwise, a life full of unhappiness was predicted.
The tragedy came one day when a couple wanted to comply with the tradition but the young woman fell into the river that caught her in a whirlpool. Given the situation, her boyfriend jumped to save her but they both drowned. After this tragedy, the tradition was questioned until it stopped being practiced.
